1	# Custom pools
2	
3	Custom pools are pools that inherit from the `worker` pool. They use any MachineConfig targeted for the `worker` pool
4	but add the ability to deploy changes only targeted at the custom pool.
5	Since a custom pool inherits from the `worker` pool, any change to the `worker` pool is going to roll out to the custom pool as well (like an OS update during an upgrade).
6	
7	**Note:** Custom pools on master nodes are not supported by MCO. For more detail, see [Custom pool on master node](#Custom-pool-on-master-node)
8	
9	## Creating a custom pool
10	
11	The first thing to do to create a custom pool is labeling the node with a custom role - in our example it will be `infra`:
12	
13	```console
14	$ oc label node ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal node-role.kubernetes.io/infra=
15	```
16	
17	```console
18	$ oc get nodes
19	NAME                                         STATUS    ROLES          AGE   VERSION
20	ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready     infra,worker   37m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
21	ip-10-0-131-9.us-west-1.compute.internal     Ready     master         43m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
22	ip-10-0-134-237.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready     master         43m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
23	ip-10-0-138-167.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready     worker         37m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
24	ip-10-0-151-146.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready     master         43m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
25	ip-10-0-152-59.us-west-1.compute.internal    Ready     worker         37m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
26	```
27	
28	When we create infra nodes, we apply them to existing worker nodes first. If you want to use the node as a purely infra node, you can remove the worker pool as follows:
29	
30	```
31	oc label node ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal node-role.kubernetes.io/worker-
32	```
33	
34	This will not change the files on the node itself: the infra pool inherits from workers by default. This means that if you add a new MachineConfig to update workers, a purely infra node will still get updated. However this will mean that workloads scheduled for workers will no longer be scheduled on this node, as it no longer has the worker label.
35	
36	Next you need to create a MachineConfigPool that contains both the `worker` role and your custom one as MachineConfig selector as follows:
37	
38	```console
39	$ cat infra.mcp.yaml
40	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
41	kind: MachineConfigPool
42	metadata:
43	  name: infra
44	spec:
45	  machineConfigSelector:
46	    matchExpressions:
47	      - {key: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role, operator: In, values: [worker,infra]}
48	  nodeSelector:
49	    matchLabels:
50	      node-role.kubernetes.io/infra: ""
51	```
52	
53	```console
54	$ oc create -f infra.mcp.yaml
55	```
56	
57	You can check that the custom MCP has now been created:
58	
59	```console
60	$ oc get mcp
61	NAME     CONFIG                                             UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED
62	infra    rendered-infra-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b    True      False      False
63	master   rendered-master-7053d8fc3619388accc12c7759f8241a   True      False      False
64	worker   rendered-worker-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b   True      False      False
65	```
66	
67	The example above makes an `infra` pool that contains all of the MachineConfigs used by the `worker` pool.
68	
69	## Deploy changes to a custom pool (optional)
70	
71	Deploying changes to a custom pool is just a matter of creating a MachineConfig that uses the custom pool name as the label (`infra` in the example):
72	
73	```console
74	$ cat infra.mc.yaml
75	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
76	kind: MachineConfig
77	metadata:
78	  labels:
79	    machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role: infra
80	  name: 51-infra
81	spec:
82	  config:
83	    ignition:
84	      version: 2.2.0
85	    storage:
86	      files:
87	      - contents:
88	          source: data:,infra
89	        filesystem: root
90	        mode: 0644
91	        path: /etc/infratest
92	```
93	
94	```console
95	$ oc create -f infra.mc.yaml
96	```
97	
98	The custom `infra` pool has now deployed your change with the `infra`-targeted MachineConfig:
99	
100	```console
101	$ oc get mcp
102	NAME     CONFIG                                             UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED
103	infra    rendered-infra-dfdfdf7e006f18cd5d29cae03f77948b    True      False      False
104	master   rendered-master-7053d8fc3619388accc12c7759f8241a   True      False      False
105	worker   rendered-worker-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b   True      False      False
106	```
107	
108	Now check that the file landed on the `infra` node:
109	
110	```console
111	$ oc get pods -n openshift-machine-config-operator -l k8s-app=machine-config-daemon --field-selector "spec.nodeName=ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal"
112	NAME                          READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
113	machine-config-daemon-vxb4c   1/1     Running   2          43m
114	```
115	
116	```console
117	$ oc rsh -n openshift-machine-config-operator machine-config-daemon-vxb4c chroot /rootfs cat /etc/infratest
118	infra
119	```
120	
121	## Removing a custom pool
122	
123	Removing a custom pool requires first to un-label each node:
124	
125	```console
126	$ oc label node ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal node-role.kubernetes.io/infra-
127	```
128	
129	Note: a node must have a role at any given time to be properly functioning. If you have infra-only nodes,
130	you should first relabel the node as worker and only then proceed to unlabel it from the infra role.
131	
132	```console
133	$ oc get nodes
134	NAME                                         STATUS   ROLES    AGE   VERSION
135	ip-10-0-130-218.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready    worker   50m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
136	ip-10-0-131-9.us-west-1.compute.internal     Ready    master   56m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
137	ip-10-0-134-237.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready    master   56m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
138	ip-10-0-138-167.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready    worker   50m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
139	ip-10-0-151-146.us-west-1.compute.internal   Ready    master   56m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
140	ip-10-0-152-59.us-west-1.compute.internal    Ready    worker   50m   v1.14.0+e020ea5b3
141	```
142	
143	The MCO is then going to reconcile the node to the `worker` pool configuration.
144	
145	```console
146	$ oc get mcp
147	NAME     CONFIG                                             UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED
148	infra    rendered-infra-dfdfdf7e006f18cd5d29cae03f77948b    True      False      False
149	master   rendered-master-7053d8fc3619388accc12c7759f8241a   True      False      False
150	worker   rendered-worker-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b   False     True       False
151	```
152	
153	As soon as the `worker` pool reconciles, you can remove the `infra` MCP and any MC created.
154	
155	```console
156	$ oc get mcp
157	NAME     CONFIG                                             UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED
158	infra    rendered-infra-dfdfdf7e006f18cd5d29cae03f77948b    True      False      False
159	master   rendered-master-7053d8fc3619388accc12c7759f8241a   True      False      False
160	worker   rendered-worker-6db67f47c0b205c26561b1c5ab74d79b   True      False      False
161	
162	$ oc delete mc 51-infra
163	machineconfig.machineconfiguration.openshift.io "51-infra" deleted
164	
165	$ oc delete mcp infra
166	machineconfigpool.machineconfiguration.openshift.io "infra" deleted
167	```
168	
169	## Custom pool on master node
170	Custom pool on a node having master role is not supported. `oc label node` will apply the new custom role to the target master node but MCO will not apply changes specific to the custom pool. Error can be seen in the Machine Config Controller pod logs. This behaviour is to make sure that control plane nodes remain stable.
171	
172	## Understanding custom pool updates
173	
174	A node can be part of at most one pool.  The MCO will roll out updates for pools independently; for example, if there is an OS update or other change that affects all pools, normally 1 node from the `master` and `worker` pool would update at the same time.  If you add an `infra` pool for example, then 1 node from that pool will also try to roll out concurrently with the `master` and `worker`.
175

1	# Summary
2	
3	Users need a way to update Kubelet configuration and Kubelet tuneables. Users can do this via MCO currently, but they need to know the correct options to use. We can hopefully help customers by documenting a CRD with the Kubelet tuneables and have the MCO use these tuneables when rendering the kubelet.conf and kubelet.system files to Ignition/disk.
4	
5	Runtime configuration is configured via Kubelet as well. The CRI-O runtime is used by default and is the recommended solution. Some customers may have Docker specific tools or monitoring products they have paid for.
6	
7	# Motivation
8	
9	The MCO contains the logic to write the kubelet.conf configuration file and kubelet.system systemd unit file to Ignition. When Ignition starts on a machine it writes these two files to configure the kubelet. When these resources change within the MachineConfig, the new configs are rewritten and the MachineConfigDaemon is instructed to reboot the machine to pick up the new configs.
10	
11	## Goals
12	
13	The following goals should not require explicit knowledge of a customer to know the right command-line or configuration options to use within the Kubelet.
14	
15	1. changing out of resource handling ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/out_of_resource_handling.html#out-of-resource-create-config](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/out_of_resource_handling.html#out-of-resource-create-config))
16	
17	2. setting a feature gate ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/install_config/configuring_ephemeral.html#ephemeral-storage-enabling-ephemeral-storage](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/install_config/configuring_ephemeral.html#ephemeral-storage-enabling-ephemeral-storage))
18	
19	3. setting cpu manager to static ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/scaling_performance/using_cpu_manager.html](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/scaling_performance/using_cpu_manager.html))
20	
21	4. setting max pods per node ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/manage_nodes.html#admin-guide-max-pods-per-node](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/manage_nodes.html#admin-guide-max-pods-per-node))
22	
23	5. configuring garbage collection ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/garbage_collection.html](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/garbage_collection.html))
24	
25	6. configuring node resources ([https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/manage_nodes.html#configuring-node-resources](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/3.11/admin_guide/manage_nodes.html#configuring-node-resources))
26	
27	## Non-Goals
28	
29	# Proposal
30	
31	Extend the Machine Config Operator to include a KubetletConfig CRD and KubeletConfigController. By using a KubeletConfig CRD there is an implicit allowlist of allowed user controlled options. Upon deleting the KubeletConfig instance the default config is restored.
32	
33	## CRD
34	
35	```
36	apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
37	kind: CustomResourceDefinition
38	metadata:
39	  name: kubeletconfigs.machineconfiguration.openshift.io
40	spec:
41	  group: machineconfiguration.openshift.io
42	  versions:
43	    - name: v1
44	      served: true
45	      storage: true
46	  scope: Cluster
47	  names:
48	    plural: kubeletconfigs
49	    singular: kubeletconfig
50	    kind: KubeletConfig
51	```
52	
53	## SPEC
54	
55	```
56	MachineConfigPoolSelector *metav1.LabelSelector
57	Runtime:
58	  Name  string
59	  Endpoint string
60	KubeletConfig
61	  [KubeletConfigurationSpec](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.11/pkg/kubelet/apis/kubeletconfig/v1beta1/types.go#L45)
62	```
63	
64	## VALIDATION
65	
66	It's important to note that, since the fields of the kubelet configuration are directly fetched from upstream the validation
67	of those values is handled directly by the kubelet. Please refer to the upstream version of the relavent kubernetes for the
68	valid values of these fields. Invalid values of the kubelet configuration fields may render cluster nodes unusable.
69	
70	## Example - Setting the Kubelet Log Level
71	This is what an example `kubelet config` CR looks like. Note: you must make sure to add a label under `matchLabels` in the KubeletConfig CR:
72	
73	```
74	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
75	kind: KubeletConfig
76	metadata:
77	  name: set-max-pods
78	spec:
79	  logLevel: 5
80	  machineConfigPoolSelector:
81	    matchLabels:
82	      pools.operator.machineconfiguration.openshift.io/worker: ""
83	  kubeletConfig:
84	    maxPods: 100
85	```
86	
87	The logLevel attribute is optional and will default to level 2. Increasing the logLevel
88	uses more IO, CPU, and resources on all the machines within the pool.
89	
90	Save your `kubeletconfig` locally, for example as maxpods.yaml
91	
92	The label in the above example corresponds to the worker MachineConfigPool. By default the master/worker
93	MachineConfigPool has labels pools.operator.machineconfiguration.openshift.io/{worker|master}: "" in OCP 4.6 and later. If you have a custom pool, or have an earlier OCP version, you can instead create a label yourself as follows:
94	
95	```
96	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
97	kind: KubeletConfig
98	metadata:
99	  name: set-max-pods
100	spec:
101	  logLevel: 5
102	  machineConfigPoolSelector:
103	    matchLabels:
104	      custom-kubelet: small-pods
105	  kubeletConfig:
106	    maxPods: 100
107	```
108	
109	To roll out the pods limit changes to all the worker nodes (can switch this to master for the master nodes), add the label that you created, here: `custom-kubelet: small-pods` under labels in the machineConfigPool config:
110	
111	```
112	oc edit machineconfigpool worker
113	```
114	
115	Snippet of the machineConfigPool config with the matching label added:
116	
117	```
118	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
119	kind: MachineConfigPool
120	metadata:
121	  creationTimestamp: 2019-04-10T16:39:39Z
122	  generation: 1
123	  labels:
124	    custom-kubelet: small-pods
125	  name: worker
126	  ...
127	```
128	Now apply the `kubeletconfig` that you created:
129	
130	```
131	$ oc apply -f maxpods.yaml
132	kubeletconfig.machineconfiguration.openshift.io/set-max-pods created
133	```
134	
135	Double check that it was created:
136	
137	```
138	$ oc get kubeletconfig
139	NAME           AGE
140	set-max-pods   6s
141	```
142	
143	Check to ensure that a new 99-worker-XXX-kubelet is created and that a new rendered worker is created:
144	
145	```
146	$ oc get machineconfigs
147	NAME                                 GENERATEDBYCONTROLLER                      IGNITIONVERSION   AGE
148	...
149	99-worker-kubelet-managed            fc45f8b73b2fc61e567f2111181d3e802f2565d7   3.1.0             7s
150	...
151	rendered-worker-45678XYZ             fc45f8b73b2fc61e567f2111181d3e802f2565d7   3.1.0             2s
152	...
153	```
154	
155	The changes should now be rolled out to each node in the worker pool via that new rendered-worker machine config. You can verify by checking
156	that the latest rendered-worker machine-config has been rolled out to the pools successfully:
157	
158	```
159	$ oc get mcp
160	NAME     CONFIG                     UPDATED   UPDATING   DEGRADED   MACHINECOUNT   READYMACHINECOUNT   UPDATEDMACHINECOUNT   DEGRADEDMACHINECOUNT   AGE
161	...
162	worker   rendered-worker-45678XYZ   True      False      False      3              3                   3                     0                      5m
163	...
164	```
165	
166	## Example - Setting the optimal system reserved
167	You can also use `KubeletConfig` to set the optimal system reserved values for memory and cpu.
168	
169	```
170	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
171	kind: KubeletConfig
172	metadata:
173	  name: dynamic-node
174	spec:
175	  autoSizingReserved: true
176	  machineConfigPoolSelector:
177	    matchLabels:
178	      pools.operator.machineconfiguration.openshift.io/worker: ""
179	```
180	
181	The `autoSizingReserved` attribute is optional and will default to the value `false`.
182	
183	Save your `KubeletConfig` locally say, kubelet-dynamic-node.yaml
184	
185	The label in the above example corresponds to the worker MachineConfigPool. Similar approach can be take to apply the `KubeletConfig` to the master or custom pool.
186	
187	## Implementation Details
188	
189	The KubeletConfigController would perform the following steps:
190	
191	1. Validate the user defined KubeletConfig
192	
193	2. Render the current MachineConfig (storage.files.contents[kubelet.conf]) into the KubeletConfiguration structure
194	
195	3. Load the KubeletConfig from the passed in Spec.KubeletConfig
196	
197	4. Use mergo to merge the two structures
198	
199	5. Serialize the KubeletConfig to json
200	
201	6. Create or Update an ignition /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf file within a 99-[role]-kubelet-managed MachineConfig
202	
203	After deletion of the KubeletConfig instance the config will be reverted to the original kubelet config.
204	
205	## Runtime Selection
206	
207	### Requirements
208	
209	First time installs will **always** install with CRI-O. After the initial install, the user will be able to switch from CRI-O to Docker, or from Docker to CRI-O if commanded to.
210	
211	Setting the options with the KubeletConfig CRD will cause the node to drain and reboot. Upon reboot, the machine will be reconfigured to use docker as the runtime via Machine Config Daemon and a rollout to each node.
212	
213	**_Note: Docker is only available in Bring-Your-Own-RHEL (User Provisioned Infrastructure) configurations._**
214	
215	TODO: Network Daemon?
216	
217	TODO: crictl configuration?
218	
219	### Runtime Partition Requirements
220	
221	There are specific partitioning schemes that are supported.
222	
223	1. Single Root Partition (/): Single partitioning scheme where everything is on a root partition
224	
225	2. Control Partition (/var): There is a root (/) partition for the filesystem, in addition to a separate /var disk or partition for runtime specific data. This scheme is useful in cases where the customer wants a small root partition or run the runtime via another disk (ie: SSD)
226	
227	3. Control and Log Partition (/var and /var/log): There is a root (/) partition for the filesystem, in addition to a separate /var and /log disk or partition. This scheme is useful in cases where the customer wants to protect from log files filling up the primary image or runtime disk.
228

1	# MachineConfigController
2	
3	## Goals
4	
5	1. Coordinate upgrade of machines to desired configurations defined by a `MachineConfig` object.
6	
7	2. Provide options to control upgrade for `sets of machines` individually.
8	
9	## Non Goals
10	
11	1. MachineConfigController is not responsible for updating the machines directly.
12	
13	2. MachineConfigController is not responsible for serving Ignition configs to new machines trying to join the cluster.
14	
15	## Sub Controllers
16	
17	1. `TemplateController` is responsible for generating the MachineConfigs for pre-defined roles of machines from internal templates based on cluster configuration.
18	
19	2. `UpdateController` is responsible for upgrading machines to desired MachineConfig by coordinating with a daemon running on each machine.
20	
21	3. `RenderController` is responsible for discovering MachineConfigs for a `Pool of Machines` and generating the static MachineConfig.
22	
23	4. `KubeletConfigController` is responsible for wrapping custom Kubelet configurations within a CRD. The available options are documented within the KubeletConfiguration (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.11/pkg/kubelet/apis/kubeletconfig/v1beta1/types.go#L45).
24

87	## MachineSets vs MachineConfigPool
88	
89	- MachineSets describe nodes with respect to cloud / machine provider. MachineConfigPool allows MachineConfigController components to define and provide status of machines in context of upgrades.
90	
91	- MachineConfigPool also allows users to configure how upgrades are rolled out to the machines in a pool.
92	
93	- NodeSelector can be replaced with reference to MachineSet.
94	
95	## TemplateController
96	
97	The TemplateController uses `internal templates` and a `configuration object` to generate OpenShift-owned MachineConfig objects for pre-defined `roles` like master, worker etc. These templates are stored in the `templates/` directory of this repository. For example, the `templates/master/00-master` directory will yield a MachineConfig object named `00-master` for the `master` role. Template variables such as `{{.EtcdCAData}}` are filled in using the controllerconfig custom resource (e.g. `controllerconfig/machine-config-controller`).
98	
99	- The TemplateController constantly reconciles the MachineConfig objects in the cluster to match its internal state (which is essentially: baked-in templates + controllerconfig). The TemplateController will overwrite any user changes of its owned objects.
100	
101	- TemplateController watches changes to the controllerconfig to generate OpenShift-owned MachineConfig objects.
102	
103	- TemplateController adds `OwnerReference` or similar annotations on its objects to declare ownership.
104	
105	## RenderController
106	
107	The RenderController generates the desired MachineConfig object based on the MachineConfigSelector defined in MachineConfigPool.
108	
109	- RenderController watches for changes on MachineConfigPool object to find all the MachineConfig objects using `MachineConfigSelector` and updating the `CurrentMachineConfig` with the generated MachineConfig.
110	
111	- RenderController watches for changes on all the MachineConfig objects and syncs all the MachineConfigPool objects with new `CurrentMachineConfig`.
112	
113	### Finding MachineConfigs
114	
115	Use kubernetes Deployment behavior for LabelSelector to find Pods.
116	
117	### Generating desired MachineConfig
118	
119	Use the merging behavior defined in MachineConfig design document [here](./MachineConfig.md#how-to-create-generated-machineconfig) to create a single MachineConfig from all the MachineConfig objects that were selected above.
120	
121	#### Ordering the MachineConfigs
122	
123	The render controller sorts all the other MachineConfigs based on the lexicographically increasing order of their `Name`. It uses the first MachineConfig in the list as the base and appends the rest to the base MachineConfig.
124	
125	## UpdateController
126	
127	The UpdateController coordinates upgrade for machines in a MachineConfigPool. UpdateController uses annotations on node objects to coordinate with the `MachineConfigDaemon` running on each machine to upgrade each machine to the desired Machine Configuration.
128	
129	UpdateController watches for changes on MachineConfigPool and runs update if,
130	
131	1. If the `.Status.CurrentMachineConfig` has been updated.
132	
133	2. If new nodes can be updated to the current configuration as new Machines are available with old configuration if permitted by `NodeLimit` or the `NodeLimit` has increased allowing more nodes to be updated.
134	
135	**Historically** the following annotations were used to coordinate between UpdateController and the MachineConfigDaemon,
136	
137	- node-configuration.v1.coreos.com/currentConfig
138	- node-configuration.v1.coreos.com/targetConfig
139	- node-configuration.v1.coreos.com/desiredConfig
140	
141	With these three fields it becomes possible to determine the update progress of the machine:
142	
143	- desiredConfig == currentConfig: The machine is up-to-date.
144	- desiredConfig != currentConfig && desiredConfig == targetConfig: The machine is not up-to-date, but is in the process of updating.
145	- desiredConfig != currentConfig && desiredConfig != targetConfig: The machine is not up-to-date and is not in the process of updating.
146	- Node is marked updated by UpdateController unless `NodeReady` is reported by kubelet.
147	
148	## UpdateController interface with MachineConfigDaemon
149	
150	Following annotations on node object will be used by UpdateController to coordinate node update with MachineConfigDaemon.
151	
152	- machine-config-daemon.v1.openshift.com/currentConfig : defines the current MachineConfig applied by MachineConfigDaemon.
153	- machine-config-daemon.v1.openshift.com/desiredConfig : defines the desired MachineConfig that need to be applied by MachineConfigDaemon
154	- machine-config-daemon.v1.openshift.com/state : defines the state of the MachineConfigDaemon, It can be done, working and degraded.
155	
156	With these three fields it becomes possible to determine the update progress of the machine:
157	
158	  a. desiredConfig == currentConfig : The machine is up-to-date.
159	
160	  b. desiredConfig != currentConfig && state == working : The machine is not up-to-date, but is in the process of updating.
161	
162	  c. desiredConfig != currentConfig && state == degraded : The machine is not up-to-date and MachineConfigDaemon cannot apply the desired configuration.
163	
164	Node is marked updated by UpdateController only when `NodeReady` is reported by kubelet when case (a) is true.
165	
166	## KubeletConfig
167	
168	The KubeletConfigController manages the KubeletConfig CRD allowing customers to manage their Feature Flags, Max Pods, and other Kubelet options.
169	
170	The `KubeletConfigController` listens for changes within KubeletConfig CRDs and merges in changes from the user.
171	
172	When the user creates a new KubeletConfig object they pass in the MachineConfigPoolSelector to target.
173	
174	The MachineConfigController performs the following operations:
175	
176	1. Validates the user defined KubeletConfig
177	1. Renders the current MachineConfig (storage.files.contents[kubelet.conf]) into the KubeletConfiguration structure
178	1. Loads the user's KubeletConfig instance
179	1. Uses mergo to merge the two structures
180	1. Serializes the KubeletConfig to json
181	1. Creates or Updates a MachineConfig (called `99-[role]-kubelet-managed`) with a new /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf
182	
183	The machine will subsequently reboot by the MachineConfigDaemon to apply the new config.

1	# Questions and answers
2	
3	The goal of this file is to have a place to easily commit answers to questions
4	in a way that's easily searchable, and can make its way into official
5	documentation later.
6	
7	## Q: When I customize the Ignition generated by the installer, how does the MCO handle that?
8	
9	This is a supported operation.  Today, the MCO does not have good support for "per node"
10	configuration, and configuring things like static IP addresses and partition layouts
11	by customizing the Ignition makes sense.
12	
13	However, it's important to understand that these custom changes are "invisible" to the
14	MCO today - they won't show up in `oc get machineconfig`.  And hence it's not
15	as straightforward to make any "day 2" changes to them.
16	
17	In the future, it's likely the MCO will gain better support for per-node configuration
18	as well as tools to more easily manipulate Ignition, so there is less need to edit the
19	Ignition JSON directly.
20	
21	## Q: Why are my workers showing older versions of RHCOS?
22	
23	Today, the MCO only blocks on upgrades of control plane nodes.  `oc get clusterversion` effectively reports the version of the control plane. 
24	
25	To watch rollout of worker nodes, you should look at `oc describe machineconfigpool/worker` (as well as other custom pools, if any).
26	
27	## Q: How does this relate to Machine API?
28	
29	There are two fundamental operators in OpenShift 4 that both include "machine" in their name:
30	
31	The Machine Config Operator (this repository) manages code and configuration "inside" the OS (and targets specifcally RHCOS).
32	
33	The [Machine API Operator](https://github.com/openshift/machine-api-operator) manages "machine" objects which represent underlying IaaS virtual ([or physical](https://github.com/openshift-metal3)) machines.
34	
35	In other words, they operate on fundamentally different levels, but they do interact.  For example, both currently will drain a node.  The MCO will drain when it's making changes, and machine API will drain when a machine object is deleted and has an associated node.
36	
37	Another linkage between the two is booting an instance; in IaaS scenarios the "user data" field (managed by machineAPI) will contain a "pointer Ignition config" that points to the Machine Config Server.
38	
39	However, these repositories have distinct teams.  Also, machineAPI is a derivative of a Kubernetes upstream project "cluster API", whereas the MCO is not.
40	
41	## Q: If I change something manually on the host, will the MCO revert it?
42	
43	Usually, no.  Today, the MCO does not try to claim "exclusive" ownership over everything on the host system; it's just not feasible to do.
44	
45	If for example you write a daemonset that writes a custom systemd unit into e.g. `/etc/systemd/system`, or do so manually via `ssh`/`oc debug node` - OS upgrades will preserve that change (via libostree), and the MCO will not revert it.  The MCO/MCD only changes files included in `MachineConfigs`, there is no code to look for "unknown" files.
46	
47	Another case today is that the SDN operator will extract some binaries from its container image and drop them in `/opt` (which is really `/var/opt`).
48	
49	Stated more generally, on an OSTree managed system, all content in `/etc` and `/var` is [preserved by default across upgrades](https://ostreedev.github.io/ostree/adapting-existing/).
50	
51	Further, rpm-ostree supports package layering and overrides - these will also be preserved by the MCO (currently).  Although note that there is no current mechanism to trigger a MCO-coordinated drain/reboot, which is particularly relevant for `rpm-ostree install/override` changes.
52	
53	If a file that *is* managed by MachineConfig is changed, the MCD will detect this and go degraded.  We go degraded rather than overwrite in order to avoid [reboot loops](https://github.com/openshift/machine-config-operator/pull/245).
54	
55	In the future, we would like to harden things more so that these things are more controlled, and ideally avoid having any persistent "unmanaged" state.  But it will take significant work to get there; and the status quo means that we can support other operators such as SDN (and e.g. [nmstate](https://github.com/nmstate/kubernetes-nmstate)) that may control parts of the host without the MCO's awareness.
56	
57	## Q: How do I debug a node failing to join the cluster?
58	
59	In clusters that are managed by the [Machine API](https://github.com/openshift/machine-api-operator/), see
60	[this question](https://github.com/openshift/machine-api-operator/blob/master/FAQ.md#i-created-a-machine-but-it-never-joined-the-cluster)
61	first.
62	
63	A node failing to join can fail broadly speaking at two separate stages; inside
64	the initramfs (Ignition), or in the real root.  If Ignition fails, at the moment
65	this requires accessing the console of the affected machine.  See also
66	[this issue](https://github.com/coreos/ignition/issues/585).
67	
68	If the system fails in the real root, and you have configured a SSH key for
69	the cluster, you should be able to `ssh` to the node.  A good first command is
70	`systemctl --failed`.  Important units to look at would be `machine-config-daemon-firstboot.service`
71	and `kubelet.service` - in general, the problem is likely to be some dependency
72	of kubelet.
73	
74	## Q: Can I use the MCO to re-partition or re-install?
75	
76	Not today.  The [MachineConfig](MachineConfig.md) doc discusses which sections
77	of the rendered Ignition can be changed, and that does not include e.g. the Ignition
78	`storage` section.  For example, you cannot currently switch an existing worker
79	node to be encrypted or use RAID after the fact - you must re-provision the system.
80	
81	The MCO also does not *currently* support explicitly re-provisioning a system "in place", however
82	this is likely to be a future feature.  For now, in machineAPI managed environments
83	you should `oc delete` the corresponding `machine` object, or in UPI installations,
84	cordon and drain the node, then delete the `node` object and re-provision.
85	
86	A further problem is that the MCO does not make it easy for *new* nodes to boot
87	in the new configuration.
88	
89	Related issues:
90	
91	 - https://github.com/openshift/machine-config-operator/issues/1619
92	 - https://github.com/openshift/machine-config-operator/pull/2035
93	
94	All this to say, it's quite hard to change storage layout with the MCO today,
95	but this is a bug.
96	
97	## Q: Why am I getting "error: No enabled repositories" from rpm-ostree?
98	
99	As of OCP 4.13 and below, when any extensions are enabled (e.g. `kernel-rt`, `usbguard` etc.)
100	the MCO will provision an rpm-md (yum/dnf) repository only at the time the MCD is making
101	changes to the host system.
102	
103	This means that any other invocations of rpm-ostree (e.g. a manual `rpm-ostree initramfs --enable`,
104	or `rpm-ostree install` etc.) won't have that repository enabled.
105	
106	In some cases, creating an empty/dummy repository in `/etc/yum.repos.d` *may* suffice to work around this bug.
107	Or, adding the RHEL UBI repository or [full RHEL entitlements](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/4.13/support/remote_health_monitoring/insights-operator-simple-access.html#insights-operator-simple-access) may work.
108	
109	In the future, the MCO may change to just leave the repository enabled and avoid this.
110	
111	However, if you're doing deep changes to the host, it may work significantly better
112	to instead switch to an [image layering](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/4.13/post_installation_configuration/coreos-layering.html) model.
113	
114	## Q: Does the MCO run on RHEL worker nodes?
115	
116	Yes, RHEL worker nodes will have a instance of the Machine Config Daemon running on them.  However, only a subset of MCO functionality is supported on RHEL worker nodes.  It is possible to create a Machine Config to write files and `systemd` units to RHEL worker nodes, but it is not possible to manage OS updates, kernel arguments, or extensions on RHEL worker nodes.
117

1	# MCO Domain Model - CRDs
2	
3	All CRDs belong to API group `machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1`. Types are defined in `vendor/github.com/openshift/api/machineconfiguration/v1/`. Compatibility Level 1 (stable within major release, min 12 months).
4	
5	## MachineConfig
6	
7	Cluster-scoped Ignition config fragment. No status subresource.
8	
9	**Spec:** `config` (Ignition RawExtension), `kernelArguments` ([]string), `extensions` ([]string), `fips` (bool), `kernelType` (default|realtime|64k-pages), `osImageURL`, `baseOSExtensionsContainerImage`.
10	
11	Multiple MachineConfigs are merged by the Render Controller into a single rendered config per pool. Merge order is deterministic (sorted by name). Conflicts in file paths use last-writer-wins.
12	
13	## MachineConfigPool (short: mcp)
14	
15	Groups nodes via `nodeSelector` and selects MachineConfigs via `machineConfigSelector`. Tracks update rollout status.
16	
17	**Key spec:** `paused` (bool), `maxUnavailable` (IntOrString), `pinnedImageSets` (max 100), `configuration` (target rendered config).
18	
19	**Key status:** `machineCount`, `updatedMachineCount`, `readyMachineCount`, `degradedMachineCount`, `conditions` (Updated, Updating, Degraded, NodeDegraded, RenderDegraded, Building, BuildSuccess, BuildFailed, etc.).
20	
21	**Validation rules:** machineCount >= updatedMachineCount, machineCount >= readyMachineCount, etc.
22	
23	Default pools: `master`, `worker`. Custom pools inherit from worker via `machineConfigSelector`.
24	
25	## ControllerConfig
26	
27	Cluster-wide configuration consumed by Template Controller to generate base MachineConfigs.
28	
29	**Key spec:** `clusterDNSIP`, `rootCAData`, `kubeAPIServerServingCAData`, `additionalTrustBundle`, `pullSecret`, `images` (map), `baseOSContainerImage`, `releaseImage`, `proxy`, `infra`, `dns`, `ipFamilies` (IPv4|IPv6|DualStack|DualStackIPv6Primary), `networkType`.
30	
31	**Status conditions:** TemplateControllerRunning, TemplateControllerCompleted, TemplateControllerFailing.
32	
33	## KubeletConfig
34	
35	Applies upstream kubelet configuration overrides per pool.
36	
37	**Key spec:** `machineConfigPoolSelector` (LabelSelector), `kubeletConfig` (RawExtension - upstream KubeletConfiguration), `autoSizingReserved` (bool), `logLevel` (0-10), `tlsSecurityProfile`.
38	
39	Controller generates a MachineConfig with the merged kubelet config and drops file at `/etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf`.
40	
41	## ContainerRuntimeConfig (short: ctrcfg)
42	
43	Tunes CRI-O and container runtime settings per pool.
44	
45	**Key spec:** `machineConfigPoolSelector`, `containerRuntimeConfig` with fields: `pidsLimit`, `logLevel` (fatal..debug), `logSizeMax` (>= 8192), `overlaySize` (default 10GB), `defaultRuntime` (crun|runc), `additionalLayerStores` (max 5), `additionalImageStores` (max 10), `additionalArtifactStores` (max 10).
46	
47	Store paths validated: absolute, 1-256 chars, pattern `^/[a-zA-Z0-9/._-]+$`, no consecutive slashes.
48	
49	## MachineConfigNode
50	
51	Per-node update tracking. Created/managed by Node Controller and updated by MCD.
52	
53	**Spec:** `configVersion.desired` (target MC ref), `pool.name`, `node.name`.
54	
55	**Status conditions:** Updated, UpdatePrepared, UpdateExecuted, UpdatePostActionComplete, UpdateComplete, Resumed, UpdateCompatible, AppliedFiles, AppliedFilesAndOS, Cordoned, Uncordoned, Drained, RebootedNode, ReloadedCRIO, PinnedImageSetsDegraded, PinnedImageSetsProgressing.
56	
57	## MachineOSConfig
58	
59	Defines on-cluster layering (OCL) build inputs for a pool.
60	
61	**Key spec:** `machineConfigPool` (ref), `buildInputs`, `containerFile` ([]ContainerFile with `content` and optional `containerfileArch`).
62	
63	## MachineOSBuild
64	
65	Tracks execution of an OCL build job.
66	
67	**Key spec:** `machineOSConfig` (ref), `machineConfig` (ref).
68	
69	**Key status:** `digestedImagePushSpec` (built image), `builder.job` (Job ref with name/namespace).
70	
71	## OSImageStream
72	
73	OS image references for the release. Managed by the osimagestream binary.
74	
75	**Status:** `defaultStream`, `availableStreams` ([]OSImageStreamSpec with `name`, `osImage`, `osExtensionsImage`).
76	
77	## PinnedImageSet
78	
79	Pre-pulls container images to nodes in matching pools.
80	
81	**Spec:** `machineConfigPoolSelector` (LabelSelector), `pinnedImages` ([]PinnedImage with `name`).
82	
83	## Feature-Gated CRD Variants
84	
85	CRDs ship in multiple variants for feature gates: Default, DevPreviewNoUpgrade, CustomNoUpgrade, TechPreviewNoUpgrade, OKD, Hypershift, SelfManagedHA. Located in `vendor/github.com/openshift/api/machineconfiguration/v1/zz_generated.crd-manifests/`.
86
